item.title: It's my cake day, so here are 50 of my favorites, part 1, most of them have sound
item.description: Link to part 2: https://imgur.com/gallery/lTTmzJo
item.dc:creator: SomeGuyWhoSaysStuff

item.title: Guys! We got married! Never been happier.
item.description: We married on June 12, 2022 Loving Day. “Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967), was a landmark civil rights decision of the U.S. Supreme Court in which the Court ruled that laws banning interracial marriage violate the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.” https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loving_v._Virginia. Edit: FRONT PAGE?! Thank you guys so much. This was hands down the happiest day of my life. We had a blast.
item.dc:creator: lacarpitrondookmarriot

item.title: Urban Farming in Toronto Season 6!
item.description:
This is April 7th, adding straw to our garlic bed. We planted 3600 garlic cloves in this backyard last year.
Garlic bed responding well to the straw mulch.
We moved! later that April, we got approved to be partners with Fresh City farms at their new downsview park urban farm. We got 1/2 acre of land! which adds up to 3/4 of an acre of urban growing space that we grow on.
The field - after soil test, we found that this soil was very poor in nutrients and organic matter (we've not seen a single earthworm! which not a good sign) So we order 180 cubic yards of compost, and ordered soil amendments to jump start this field.
Our old faithful greenhouse. This 50ft greenhouse is incredible how it produces so much food. This greenhouse, my partners 40ft and our microgreens room (you'll see it later) feeds 50+ homes for 3+ weeks. This month, we have lettuce, salad mix (a collection of mixed veggie greens) radish, cilantro, arugula and spinach. The kale you see on your right was over wintered and just came back to life in spring. It was the sweetest kale I've ever tasted!
Kale gone, now the lettuce has room to grow
First view of the field after it was disked. Disking turns over the top layer of the soil so to introduce organic matter as the vegetation decomposes. What you don't see here is how HARD! this soil is. Its clay, very compacted clay - if you put a fork into it, it wouldn't even go in! This is when we knew we had A LOT! of work on our hands.
See what I mean? this process is called bed hilling - the idea is to created a raised bed by digging trenches, and scooping the soil from the trench onto the new bed. We did 5 beds that weekend - it was so hard!
Our first inaugural compost dump - this was our first 60 cubic yard order. Each bed required 12-16 wheelbarrows - each bed is 80ft - we have 45 beds - you do the math - yes, we to move all that Now what you don't see here is that this is May and we are weeks away from our first delivery and after which, we'll need to put transplants onto this plot! We have a set schedule when plants need to be in so as to keep up with the deliveries for our customers - the pressure is on!
Testing the compost size and how the system will work
This is when knowing friends with tools help! This machine is called a BCS - its a walk behind tractor. The attachment in the back is a trench digger! Lets just say, this machine saved us a month worth of labour making and shaping our raised beds!
Our transplants screaming to be planted. Here we have Zinnias, kale, parsley, onions, chard and beets
Our first mission critical beds are built! We add woodchips on the paths because not only do they help with weed suppression, they are also great for holding water, adding organic matter, and mycelium!
Transplants are now in! This zinnia was the first to flower - it made my day to see life starting to take hold here.
in May CBC National came to visit the project - here's the clip https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F7TTlChiMxs&ab_channel=CBCNews%3ATheNational
May 21st, very productive day with planting our bok choy.
Same day, this is when that freak storm came through. It decimated our 40ft greenhouse and totally destroyed the structure. This was a hard day!
Our microgreen grow room - we doubled the space over the winter to increase our growing capacity.
In June a killdeer moved in! she laid her eggs right on the zinnia bed and yells at me every time I was past - so we had a little talk and agreed that we share the space - no need to scream at each other
June 3rd progress - slowly slowly making new beds, by now we have 18 beds, each with 12 wheelbarrow loads of compost
Shameless selfie - I was so happy this day to accomplish what we had done so far. Even though we had so much left to do
We ordered 60 more cubic yards of compost. This type of compost will help more for our carrots and direct seeding crops.
June 8th progress - the transplants are responding well to their new home. By now, we've added our zucchinis and need to make more beds for tomatoes and eggplants
June 10th, its now hot enough to start adding the hot crops. We reset the greenhouse, removing the salad mix, radish, arugula and spinach to add cucumbers.
Our tomatoes now ready to be transplanted
Progress so far - 37 beds made 444 wheelbarrows of compost later
I love looking at this - still no earthworms but there's hope! A question where our food goes - we had initially started with 25 customers and now we have a capacity for 100 - and co-op group of farmers to do 100 more We also have a project where our food goes to a local food bank and any extra goes to Foodshare Toronto who subsidize the produce for locations where income disparity is rampant
item.dc:creator: FarmerNjau

item.title: 'Look for the helpers.' - Mr. Rogers
item.description: Given the events of recent weeks we could all use a reminder that true heroes do exist.
item.dc:creator: Pastapersonisevil

item.title: Something something something something, something something something something something something something something something, something something something something something something something something something something, SHALL NOT BE INFRINGED
item.dc:creator: archafuri

item.title: Peter Dinklage
item.description:
His birthday is June 11, 1969. He is actually one of my favorite actors. I love him most in "The Station Agent", but he is awesome in everything he does.
Dinklage was in a Punk Funk Rap band.
He is a vegetarian.
Peter once saw a stranger die.
He was the only GOT actor to win an Emmy for his work on the show.
item.dc:creator: mfrybeasley

item.title: Col Shaw was a fine man.
item.description: Now, here is an honorable white soldier who is color-blind. Imagine a White Man leading Black Troops during the Civil War. Extraordinary. Deepest Respect.
item.dc:creator: Ice2Ice2

item.title: Bryan Cranston & Aaron Paul handing out samples of their tequila in Costco
item.description: https://www.yahoo.com/lifestyle/bryan-cranston-aaron-paul-might-163000398.html
item.dc:creator: mfrybeasley

item.title: Still alive, y’all
item.description:
It’s-a me, your boy Eli. I do have very bad connection. Imgur won’t let me post anything. Maybe I should reinstall it or smth. If it will get posted, It’ll be amazing.
Everyone keeps asking about reliable guys to donate. These dudes are the best so far. Their help and support is so powerful, that it’s literally changing the war. I’m very thankful to them. Look them up, or follow the link https://www.comebackalive.in.ua/
This is a powerful antirussian OSINT collective. They’re very helpful to our intelligence, they’re identifying russian war criminals and occupants. Look them up, they do have a patreon
This is Zhuzha, she’s a nice old girl
We gave her some armor)
My nocturnal friend
This was our dining room
Reminds me of my flag
Sunrise near Kyiv, about a month ago
My post over there
This is a deck of cards with our top enemies on them. It was a gift from our intelligence guys for my help with some stuff)
Oh, I’ve bought myself a watch. It’s very important, but expensive. This one was advised to me by German SOF dude.
After almost three months of war, we finally got our first real training. Small unit tactics, clearing building, etc. stuff like that is important and appreciated. If you have any kind of knowledge about stuff like that – please share.
I haven’t slept enough in 100 days or so
This was my crib. I even made some floorboards
This was my crib in a different place
This is Oleg. Even though other guys calling their weapon by their GF’s names, I called him Oleg. His upgrades were expensive, but worthy. Also I don’t know anyone called Oleg.
These are some of the “presents” we receive from our russian guests.
This is me moving to my next post
This is what death sounds like. The video is from my friend, my platoon mate Bandera. Many of my friends and acquaintances are killed or injured. But we’re still fighting. If you are supporting us, our fight for freedom and democracy — I’m grateful. If you’re not, then please consider to do so. We need your help. It shouldn’t be materialistic or anything. Twits, posts, signed petitions – everything helps.
item.dc:creator: EliBLifter

item.dc:creator: wiertje

item.title: That's not how any of this works.
item.dc:creator: mfrybeasley

item.title: Bottom Shelf Meme Dump
item.description:
I'm on a long road trip with my husband headed to Mississippi.
We met back in 2012 when we were both stationed there.
I only had 3 weeks left in the military when I had crossed paths with him.
I was 19 and he was 21. We were young and stupid as fuck.
I spent so much time with him in those 3 weeks, and when I moved back to Tennessee, we continued to talk for hours everyday on Skype.
After 3 months of talking, he told me he was going to be deployed to Okinawa soon. He invited me to join him on his leave to his home in Texas to introduce me to his family before he left for Japan.
I said "No".
Not because I didn't want to go, but because I was broke and couldn't afford the trip.
He offered to pay my way, and again I said "No". I didn't want him to spend his money on me, and I just didn't have money like that at the time. It wound up being our first true argument, but he insisted, and I eventually agreed to go.
I drove from Tennessee to Mississippi to meet him, and he proposed. Without any consideration at all, I just said "sure." Then he and I drove to his parent's house in Texas.
His mother did not trust me, and why would she? I had only dated her son for 3 months, and we're engaged all of a sudden. People either assumed I was in it for the money, or that I must have been pregnant.
Nope. Neither. I was just stupid as fuck.
The plan was to at least wait until he got back from deployment 9 months later before we tied the knot. However, the more we were reminded how dumb we were acting, the more we wanted to just take the plunge.
So we eloped at the local courthouse. That was nine years and 364 days ago.
These past 10 years with him have been so much fun. We grew up together. We've grown from being stupid punk ass kids to being stupid punks ass adults, and I couldn't imagine how my life would have turned out if I had never crossed paths with him that one day.
So, here we are, driving back to Mississippi to celebrate our 10 year anniversary in the place where it all began.
This time, we're staying on a dope little house boat, and I'm so fucking excited about it.
I'm excited to see what these next 10 years are going to look like as well.
& if you hated this story, at least you can enjoy some stolen memes!
Taxi tax.
item.dc:creator: BottomShelfVodka
